segments.inferno-22-g18: 'Pur' is an adverb meaning 'still' or 'yet' at the start of the clause; 'a la pegola' is a prepositional phrase ('to/on the pegola') marking the location/focus of attention (pegola = pitch/sticky surface); 'era' is the imperfect indicative of 'essere' ('was'); 'la mia ’ntesa' is the subject complement: the feminine possessive 'mia' + archaic/truncated '’ntesa' (intesa) meaning roughly 'watchfulness/attention' (the initial vowel is elided for metrical reasons).
segments.inferno-22-g19: 'Per' introduces purpose here ('in order to'); the infinitive 'veder' depends on it ('to see'); 'de la bolgia' = 'of the bolgia' (bolgia = the infernal trench/ditch) and 'ogne contegno' ('every contegno' = every gesture or bearing) is the direct object of 'veder'; the coordination 'e de la gente ch’entro v’era incesa' adds 'and of the people who/which were incesa within it' — 'ch’' is the elided 'che' introducing a relative clause, 'v’' is the adverbial 'vi' ('there') attached before the verb 'era' (imperfect), and 'incesa' is a past participle/adjectival predicate meaning 'inflamed/aflame', linked to 'era'.
segments.inferno-22-g20: 'Come' starts a simile meaning 'as' or 'like'; 'i dalfini' is the simple plural subject 'the dolphins.' This short phrase sets up the comparison for the following clause.
segments.inferno-22-g21: This is a temporal clause starting with 'quando' ('when'); 'fanno' is present indicative, 3rd person plural ('they make'); 'segno' is 'a sign' (here 'signal') and the indirect object 'a’ marinar' = 'ai marinai' ('to the sailors') uses the contracted preposition 'a’'. 'Con l’arco de la schiena' is an instrumental phrase ('with the arch/curve of the back'). The relative/result clause introduced by 'che' uses the archaic subjunctive-looking form 's’argomentin' (s' + verb + -in ending is a poetic/archaic plural ending) meaning 'they demonstrate/argue to themselves or make clear'; it governs the infinitive phrase 'di campar lor legno' ('that their ship may survive' — 'campar' as infinitive 'to survive' and 'lor legno' = 'their ship', with the archaic possessive 'lor' preceding the noun).
segments.inferno-22-g22: 'Talor' means 'sometimes' and 'così' 'thus/so'; together they form an adverbial phrase that transitions from the dolphin simile back to the sinners, functioning like 'at times in this way.'
segments.inferno-22-g23: 'Ad' (a + infinitive) introduces purpose and governs the following infinitive 'alleggiar' ('to lighten, relieve'); 'la pena' is the object ('the pain/punishment'), so the whole phrase reads 'to lighten the pain.'
segments.inferno-22-g24: 'Mostrav’' is a contracted/poetic form of the imperfect 'mostrava' (3rd singular imperfect) with the final vowel elided for the verse; it functions here as a habitual/simple past action 'showed.' 'Alcun de’ peccatori' = 'some of the sinners' (note the contracted 'de’' = 'dei'). '’l dosso' is 'the back' (article + noun). The coordinate verb 'e nascondea' ('and hid') is another imperfect form, showing a repeated or quick action; the idiom 'in men che non balena' literally 'in less than (it) flashes' uses 'che' as a than/temporal conjunction and 'balena' ('flashes') to convey 'in less than a flash.'
segments.inferno-22-g25: E come = 'And as/like': a simple comparative connector. a l’orlo de l’acqua d’un fosso = prepositional phrase ‘at the edge of the water of a ditch’; note the elisions (a + il -> a l’, di + un -> d’un). stanno = 3rd person plural present of stare, used here as a locative 'stand/are situated'. pur col muso fuori = 'with only/merely the snout out' where pur = 'only/still' and col = con + il 'with the'.
segments.inferno-22-g26: sì che = 'so that' introducing result. celano = 3rd person plural present of celare 'they conceal'. i piedi e l’altro grosso = 'the feet and the other large [mass/part]' — altro + grosso = the rest/other bulk of the body.
segments.inferno-22-g27: sì stavan = 'thus they stood' (sì = 'thus/so'; stavan = imperfect/pluperfect‑style form of stare, 'were standing'). d’ogne parte = 'on every side' (ogne = every, parte = side/place). i peccatori = the sinners (noun plus article).
segments.inferno-22-g28: ma come = 'but as' (contrastive). s’ appressava = reflexive/pronominal form of appressare in the imperfect 'approached' (s’ = reflexive/clitic); Barbariccia is the subject (proper name).
segments.inferno-22-g29: così si ritraén = 'thus they drew back/withdrew' (si = reflexive; ritraén is an archaic/poetic form of ritrare/ritrae (draw back) used for metre). sotto i bollori = 'under the boilings' — i bollori means the seething/boiling patches of water.
segments.inferno-22-g30: I’ = dialectal/io 'I' (subject pronoun). vidi = passato remoto of vedere, 'I saw' (simple past). The phrase is a simple main clause introducing the speaker's vision.
segments.inferno-22-g31: e anco = 'and also/even'; il cor = 'the heart' (subject of the following verb). me n’accapriccia = me + clitic n’ + accapriccia (3rd sing.); accapricciare here means 'to make tremble/shudder' or 'to appall' and the clitics me n’ indicate 'to me of it' (i.e. 'it makes my heart shudder').
segments.inferno-22-g32: uno aspettar così = 'one waiting thus' or 'each one waited thus' — uno is an indefinite/collective 'one' and aspettar is the bare infinitive used substantively ('a waiting' / 'waiting'), describing the posture/behaviour.
segments.inferno-22-g33: com’ elli ’ncontra = 'as (they) encounter/meet' — com’ = come 'as', elli is an archaic form for 'they/one' used impersonally, ncontra (incontrare) = 'meet/encounter/come up against'. ch’una rana rimane e l’altra spiccia = 'that one frog remains and the other darts off' (rimane = stays, spiccia = hurries off/darts away).
segments.inferno-22-g34: e Graffiacan = 'and Graffiacan' (proper name, the demon Graffiacan). This links the previous simile to the action of that named demon.
segments.inferno-22-g35: che li era più di contra = 'who was more opposite them / who stood more against them' — che introduces a relative clause; era = imperfect 'was'; più di contra = 'more to the front/opposite' (di here part of the comparative idiom). li functions as a clitic referring to the sinners ('to/for them').
segments.inferno-22-g36: li arruncigliò le ’mpegolate chiome = 'he ruffled/entangled for them their pitch‑covered locks' — li = 'them' (dative/clitic referring to the sinners), arruncigliò = past tense 'he hooked/ruffled', le ’mpegolate chiome = 'the tarred/pitch‑covered locks' (le = feminine plural article). e trassel sù = 'and pulled them up' — trassel is an archaic/metre‑shaped form meaning 'pulled (them) up' and sù = 'up'.
segments.inferno-22-g37: che mi parve una lontra = 'so that he seemed to me an otter' — che introduces the result clause, mi is the dative 'to me', parve = passato remoto 'seemed', una lontra = 'an otter'.
segments.inferno-22-g139: 'Ond'' is an archaic/poetic contraction (from onde/ond') meaning 'whereupon' or 'then'; it introduces what follows. 'ei' is the subject pronoun 'he' placed after the connective (inversion typical of older/poetic Italian).
segments.inferno-22-g140: The relative clause begins with 'ch’' (= che, 'who/that'), linking to 'ei'. 'avea' is the imperfect of avere ('had'), marking an ongoing or habitual state in the past. 'lacciuoli' is a diminutive plural meaning little snares/tricks. The prepositional phrase 'a gran divizia' qualifies the quantity: 'a' here introduces the measure/extent, 'gran' is an adjective 'great', and 'divizia' means 'abundance'.
segments.inferno-22-g141: 'rispuose' is the simple past (passato remoto) of rispondere: 'answered' or 'replied'. The colon that follows introduces direct speech.
segments.inferno-22-g142: Direct speech begins. 'Malizioso' is an adjective meaning 'malicious'/'full of malice'; 'son' is the present tense of essere used here with first person 'io' and means 'I am' (older form 'son' instead of 'sono'); 'troppo' is an adverb 'too' (excessively). Note the subject pronoun 'io' is explicit for emphasis.
segments.inferno-22-g143: 'Quand'' (quand' = quando) is 'when'. 'io' is the subject pronoun 'I' (emphatic). 'procuro' is present tense of procurare: here best rendered 'I set about / I seek to bring about' (Dante uses it to mean actively causing or contriving). The preposition 'a' introduces the object/aim 'mia maggior trestizia' — 'mia' = 'my'; 'maggior' = comparative/superlative 'greater'/'greatest' (here 'greatest'); 'trestizia' is an older/poetic noun meaning 'wickedness' or 'malice'. The whole clause expresses 'when I set about my greatest wickedness.'
segments.inferno-22-g144: 'Alichin' (Alichino) is the subject; 'non si tenne' is a reflexive past statement where 'tenne' is the passato remoto (or simple past) of 'tenere' and with 'si' means 'restrained himself' — so 'non si tenne' = 'he could not hold himself back' or 'he lost his restraint.' The final 'e' simply links to the following phrase.
segments.inferno-22-g145: Adverbial phrase 'di rintoppo a li altri' modifies the verb that follows: 'di rintoppo' = 'as/for an obstacle/check' and 'a li altri' = 'to the others' (the older form 'li altri' = modern 'gli altri'). The whole phrase means roughly 'as a check/retort directed at the others.'
segments.inferno-22-g146: 'disse' is the passato remoto (simple past) of 'dire' = 'he said'; 'a lui' is the indirect object 'to him' (introducing reported/direct speech). The sequence 'disse a lui:' introduces the following quotation.
segments.inferno-22-g147: This is the protasis of a conditional: 'Se' = 'if' (introduces the condition); 'tu' subject pronoun 'you'; 'ti' reflexive pronoun; 'cali' is 2nd person singular present of 'calare' (in older Dantesque spelling) meaning 'you descend' or 'you go down'. The construction expresses 'If you go down...'.
segments.inferno-22-g148: 'io' = 'I' (subject); 'non' negative; 'ti' direct/indirect object 'you'; 'verrò' is future indicative 1st singular ('I will come'); 'dietro' = 'behind/after'; 'di gualoppo' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with a rush/at a gallop' (gualoppo = eagerness/gallop). Together: 'I will not come after you in a rush.'
segments.inferno-22-g149: 'ma' = 'but'; 'batterò' is future 1st singular of 'batterare' = 'I will beat/flap'; 'sovra' = 'on/over'; 'la pece' = 'the pitch/tar' (pece = sticky pitch); 'l’ali' = 'the wings' (l' alias the plural article + 'ali'). The image is 'I will beat my wings on the pitch' (i.e. flap them on the tar), describing what he will do instead of following at a gallop.
segments.inferno-22-g150: Lascisi is an archaic, enclitic form used as a command meaning “let (it) be left” / “let go” (imperative sense). The clitic -si is reflexive/impersonal and ’l is the elided article il; collo is the direct object “the neck.” The whole phrase functions as an imperative: “let go of the neck.”
segments.inferno-22-g151: Sia is the present subjunctive of essere used jussively/optatively here: “let it be.” La ripa scudo is a predicate construction with scudo as predicate nominative (the bank serving as shield). The conjunction e simply links this clause to the previous imperative.
segments.inferno-22-g152: A + infinitive (a veder) expresses purpose: “in order to see.” Se introduces an indirect yes/no question (“whether”). Tu sol più di noi vali is a comparative clause: sol (archaic for solo) emphasizes “you alone,” più di marks the comparison “more than,” and vali is present indicative 2nd person of valere meaning “be worth / be stronger / prevail.”
segments.inferno-22-g153: A vocative address: O tu che leggi = “O you who read.” Che introduces a relative clause modifying tu; leggi is present indicative 2nd person singular (“you read”).
segments.inferno-22-g154: Udirai is the future 2nd person singular of udire: “you will hear.” Nuovo ludo is the direct object: ludo = “game/ jest/ sport”; nuovo qualifies it as “a new/strange” one. The colon marks the following narrative detail.
segments.inferno-22-g155: Ciascun is the subject “each one.” Da l’altra costa is a prepositional phrase indicating origin/point of view (“from the other shore/bank”); note Dante’s article contraction l’ = il. Li occhi precedes the verb as the direct object (“his eyes”), and volse is the perfective past (passato remoto) of volgere/volcere, “turned.” The syntax places the object before the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-22-g156: Quel is a demonstrative pronoun “that one,” and prima is an adverb here meaning “first” (i.e., he did so first). Together they identify which of the combatants turned his eyes first.
segments.inferno-22-g157: Ch’ is the contracted che introducing the relative clause “who/that.” A ciò fare is a purpose/infinitival expression meaning “in doing this” or “about doing this” (a + demonstrative + infinitive). Era is the imperfect of essere (“was”) and più crudo is a comparative: “the more cruel/harsh/savage,” describing that one’s temperament toward the action.
segments.inferno-22-g52: Lo + noun (Lo duca mio) is the poetic/older article + noun order meaning 'the duke, my [duke]' with mio as a possessive after the noun. li is the clitic pronoun 'to him' placed before the reflexive s' (s' = si, oneself) contracted onto the verb; accostò is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of accostare 'approach', and allato is an adverb/old form meaning 'beside/at his side'.
segments.inferno-22-g53: domandollo is domandò (passato remoto, 'he asked') with the enclitic lo ('him') attached; ond (short for 'onde') means 'whence/from where' introducing the indirect question; ei is the archaic subject pronoun 'he' (egli); fosse is the imperfect subjunctive of essere used in the reported/indirect question ('where he was').
segments.inferno-22-g54: e = 'and'; quei is a demonstrative 'that one' used as the subject ('that one replied'); rispuose is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of rispondere 'he replied/answered'.
segments.inferno-22-g55: I' is the contracted form of io (the first‑person pronoun) in older/poetic spelling; fui is passato remoto of essere 'I was'; del regno di Navarra = 'of the kingdom of Navarre'; nato is the past participle of nascere and with fui gives the sense 'I was born' (a resultative/periphrastic formation used here to state birthplace).
segments.inferno-22-g56: Mia madre is the subject 'my mother'; a servo d’un segnor means 'as/into the service of a lord' (a + noun indicates role/position); mi is the direct object 'me' and puose is passato remoto (older form of pose/pose/put) meaning 'she put/placed'.
segments.inferno-22-g57: che introduces a relative clause 'who/which/that'; m' is the enclitic me 'me'; avea is the imperfect auxiliary (had) and generato is the past participle of generare, together forming the trapassato (pluperfect) 'had begotten'; d’un ribaldo = 'by/of a rogue' (ribaldo is the father described).
segments.inferno-22-g58: distruggitor is a substantive 'destroyer'; di sé is a reflexive genitive 'of himself' and di sue cose 'and of his things' completes the description 'a destroyer of himself and of his own possessions.' The phrase is compact and nominal, typical of Dante's poetic apposition.
segments.inferno-22-g59: Poi = 'then'/'afterwards'; fui is passato remoto 'I was'; famiglia here means 'household' (i.e., I was part of the household of the good king Tebaldo), del buon re Tebaldo is the genitive specification.
segments.inferno-22-g60: quivi = 'there'; mi misi a far is the idiom 'mi misi a' (mettersi a) + infinitive meaning 'I set myself to doing' and misi is passato remoto of mettersi (here 'I set myself'); far is the infinitive 'to do/make'; baratteria means corrupt dealings or graft (here 'corruption').
segments.inferno-22-g61: di ch’io rendo ragione is the fixed expression 'di che io rendo ragione' meaning 'of which I give an account/explain'; rendo ragione is present indicative 'I give an account' (here in direct speech); in questo caldo = 'in this heat' (caldo as noun 'the heat', i.e. the present hot place).
segments.inferno-22-g62: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking this clause to what precedes; 'Cirïatto' is the proper name (subject) introduced in nominative/vocative position and will be the agent of the following actions. The diaeresis on ï is orthographic/metrical and does not affect grammar.
segments.inferno-22-g63: This is a relative clause modifying Cirïatto: 'a cui' = 'to whom/whose' (relative construction) + 'di bocca uscia' = 'issued from (his) mouth', where 'uscia' is an archaic/Dantesque form of the verb 'uscire' ('issued/came out'). 'd'ogne parte' is an adverbial phrase 'from every side', and 'una sanna come a porco' is the noun phrase 'a tusk like a boar's' (comparison introduced by 'come a'). Word order here is more compressed than modern Italian but functions as 'from whose mouth on every side came a tusk like a boar's.'
segments.inferno-22-g64: 'li' is an unstressed clitic pronoun meaning 'to him'/'to them' (indirect object) placed before the verb; 'fé' is the passato remoto (past) of 'fare' ('he made/caused'); 'sentir' is an infinitive used with 'fare' in the causative construction 'fe' + infinitive = 'caused to hear/feel/notice'. 'come l’una sdruscia' = 'how one of them slips/tilts' (l'una refers back to one of the tusks); 'sdruscia' is an older/poetic verb meaning 'slips/ slides/leans'.
segments.inferno-22-g38: I’ is an archaic contraction of io (subject pronoun) placed before the verb. sapea is the imperfect indicative of sapere (I knew / I used to know). già is an adverb 'already'. The prepositional phrase di tutti quanti ’l nome = 'the name of all of them' (di + tutti quanti = of every one; ’l is the elided article il before nome).
segments.inferno-22-g39: sì = 'so/thus'. li is a clitic object pronoun here ('them') placed before the verb; notai is passato remoto (I noted/observed). quando introduces the temporal clause 'when'. fuorono eletti is a passive construction: fuorono (archaic/full literary form of furono) = 'they were' (from essere) + eletti = 'chosen/elected'.
segments.inferno-22-g40: e poi = 'and then/afterwards'. ch’e’ is a contracted introducer (che + breviation) introducing the subordinate clause. si chiamaro is the pronominal verb chiamarsi in the passato remoto, 3rd person plural: 'they called themselves' / 'they called out' (si marks the reflexive/reciprocal pronominal form). The whole clause links temporally to the previous 'when they were chosen'.
segments.inferno-22-g41: attesi is the speaker's passato remoto of attendere, 'I waited/expected'. come is an adverb meaning 'how' or 'in what way'; the phrase attesi come is elliptical for 'I waited to see how (it would be)'.
segments.inferno-22-g42: O is the vocative interjection 'O/Oh' addressing someone; Rubicante is a proper name (vocative). The comma marks direct address.
segments.inferno-22-g43: fa che is an imperative/causative idiom (literally 'do that' / 'see to it that') introducing a dependent clause expressing a commanded result. tu is the explicit subject 'you'. The first li is a clitic object pronoun 'them' (referring to the claws) placed before metti; metti is 2nd-person singular present (put). li unghioni is a noun phrase with the article li 'the' + unghioni 'large claws'. a dosso means 'onto (his) back' or 'on top of'.
segments.inferno-22-g44: sì che = 'so that' introducing the purpose/result clause. tu lo scuoi: tu (you) + lo (him) + scuoi (present form of scuoiar — to flay). With the preceding fa che the verb scuoi appears as the commanded/resulting action (imperative/subjunctive-like sense in this older usage).
segments.inferno-22-g45: This segment is punctuation (a comma) marking a pause between the speech and the reaction that follows; it has no lexical content.
segments.inferno-22-g46: gridavan is the imperfect/poetic third-person plural of gridare, 'they were shouting/cried out'. tutti insieme = 'all together/at once'. i maladetti = 'the accursed ones' (definite article + participial adjective used as a noun). The verb-first order is normal in older/literary Italian.
segments.inferno-22-g47: E io: = 'And I:' — simple introductory formula, io is the subject 'I'.
segments.inferno-22-g48: «Maestro mio, = vocative 'my master' (Maestro addressed directly; mio is the possessive 'my').
segments.inferno-22-g49: fa, = imperative (or present) of fare, 'do (it)' — a direct plea/command to the master; fa is the archaic/poetic form used for metre instead of fai.
segments.inferno-22-g50: se tu puoi, = 'if you can' — se = 'if' (introducing a conditional), tu = subject pronoun 'you', puoi = present indicative 'you can'.
segments.inferno-22-g51: che tu sappi chi è lo sciagurato venuto a man de li avversari suoi = 'that you may find out who is the wretch who has come into the hands of his enemies.' che introduces the subordinate purpose/result clause; sappi is either subjunctive or imperative‑like 'you learn/find out' (here a desiderative/request sense); chi = 'who', è = 'is', lo sciagurato = 'the wretch', venuto = past participle 'come' (has come), a man = 'into the hand/power', de li avversari suoi = 'of his enemies' (de = of).
segments.inferno-22-g192: The coordinate conjunction E = "and" links this clause to the previous action. Noi is the (redundant/emphatic) subject pronoun and lasciammo is the passato remoto (1st person plural) of lasciare, a narrative past meaning "we left/abandoned." Lor is the unstressed object pronoun (poetic post-verbal form of loro = "them") attached after the verb. Così modifies the following past participle ’mpacciati, which is the masculine plural past participle impacciati used predicatively (= "entangled/encumbered"); the initial i is dropped and marked by the apostrophe (a poetic elision to fit meter).
segments.inferno-22-g79: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this speech to the narrative; quelli is a demonstrative pronoun here used as a third-person reference 'that one' or 'he', introducing reported speech (hence the colon).
segments.inferno-22-g80: I’ is an archaic, elided form of io ('I'); mi is the reflexive/clitic pronoun used with the pronominal verb partirsi; partii (implied in the segment) is the passato remoto 1st-person of partirsi, 'I departed/left.'
segments.inferno-22-g81: poco è is an idiomatic temporal phrase: poco (adverb 'a little') + è (present indicative of essere) yields 'it is little [time ago]' → 'a short while ago' or 'not long ago.' The order is inverted for the idiom.
segments.inferno-22-g82: da is the preposition 'from'; un is an indefinite pronoun 'a certain one/someone' ('a man'); che is the relative pronoun 'who/that'; fu is passato remoto of essere 'was' (here indicating origin or belonging); di là vicino is an adverbial phrase 'from over there nearby' (di + là + vicino).
segments.inferno-22-g83: Così 'thus/so' introduces the manner; foss' is an apocopated form of fossi/fosse (imperfect subjunctive of essere) used idiomatically here to express a counterfactual or potential past state ('I would have been'); io is the subject pronoun 'I'; ancor = ancora 'still'; con lui 'with him'; coperto is the past participle of coprire used predicatively ('covered').
segments.inferno-22-g84: ch' is the contracted che introducing a result/consequence clause ('so that'); the following i' (here representing io) is the subject 'I' (elided form of io used in older/poetic Italian); non temerei uses temerei, the conditional tense ('I would fear' → 'I would not fear' with non), so temerei expresses hypothetical/potential fear; unghia and uncino are concrete nouns 'claw' and 'hook', joined by né 'nor'.
segments.inferno-22-g85: Sentence-final punctuation: a full stop marking the end of the speech and sentence; there are no lexical items to analyze in this segment.
segments.inferno-22-g86: E is the conjunction 'and' continuing the narrative; Libicocco is the proper name of the speaker; Troppo functions adverbially 'too much' modifying the verb that follows; avem is an archaic/clitic form of abbiamo (we have) used as the auxiliary in the perfect construction; sofferto is the past participle 'suffered' (we have suffered).
segments.inferno-22-g87: disse is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of dire, 'he said', reporting Libicocco's utterance; the semicolon that follows links to the subsequent action.
segments.inferno-22-g88: e 'and' links actions; preseli is a past tense form (prese) with an enclitic pronoun (-li) attached, functioning as 'he seized/took'; ’l is the contracted definite article lo 'the' before braccio; braccio 'arm'; col is the contraction of con + il 'with the'; runciglio is a noun 'hook/talon' denoting the instrument used.
segments.inferno-22-g89: sì che is a connective phrase 'so that' introducing a result clause (sì = 'so/thus', che = 'that').
segments.inferno-22-g90: stracciando is the present gerund of stracciare ('tearing'), indicating a simultaneous action or manner ('and, tearing ...') and modifying the main verb that follows.
segments.inferno-22-g91: ne is the partitive/genitive clitic 'of/from it' referring back to the arm; portò is passato remoto (3rd person singular) of portare 'he carried/brought/took away' (here 'carried off'); un is the indefinite article 'a'; lacerto here means 'a piece/fragment' (a chunk torn off).
segments.inferno-22-g1: Vidi is the passato remoto (I saw) and governs the following infinitive clause. The sequence 'cavalier muover campo' places the (poetic) plural noun cavalier before the infinitive muover (to move/take), with campo as its object; English idiom renders this as 'see knights take the field.' Note the archaic/poetic shapes (cavalier without -i).
segments.inferno-22-g2: Cominciare and far are infinitives coordinated and understood as additional actions witnessed by vidi (I saw them begin ... and make ...). Stormo is a collective noun here meaning a skirmish/troop; lor is an enclitic pronoun attached to far and functions as a possessive/indirect element ('their' / 'for them'); mostra is a noun ('a show' or 'display').
segments.inferno-22-g3: This continues the series of infinitives governed by vidi: partir (to depart/leave) occurs in the infinitive and talvolta (sometimes) is an adverb modifying it. Per expresses purpose/reason here ('for' their scampo), and loro scampo is a possessive noun phrase meaning 'their escape/means of saving themselves.'
segments.inferno-22-g4: The order 'corridor vidi' is inverted for poetic effect (noun before verb) but still reads 'I saw raiders' (corridor as plural/poetic). Per la terra vostra is a prepositional phrase meaning 'through your land'; vostra is a possessive adjective agreeing with terra.
segments.inferno-22-g5: O is the vocative/exclamatory particle addressing the people named; Aretini is the vocative plural 'people of Arezzo.'
segments.inferno-22-g6: Vidi again governs the following infinitive phrase; gir (an archaic infinitive form of girare/andare) means 'to go/ride about,' and gualdane names the Guelph bands (poetic plural). The structure is 'I saw ... ride about.'
segments.inferno-22-g7: Fedir is an archaic infinitive (related to ferire/attaccare) and here means to strike/attack; both fedir and correr are infinitives coordinated and relate back to the witnessing verb vidi. Torneamenti and giostra are nouns referring to tournaments/jousts; 'correr giostra' is an idiom meaning 'to take part in the tilt/run the lists.'
segments.inferno-22-g8: Quando introduces a temporal clause ('when' / 'sometimes'), and con + trombe indicates the instruments/accoutrements that accompanied the movement. The line is elliptical: the verb already supplied by the earlier vidi is understood here.
segments.inferno-22-g9: Parallel to the previous clause: 'and when with bells'—quando is the temporal marker and con specifies accompaniment (bells as part of the display or signal).
segments.inferno-22-g10: A repeated prepositional pattern (con ... e con ...) lists further accompaniments: tamburi (drums) and cenni (signals). Di castella specifies origin/source of the signals ('from/of castles').
segments.inferno-22-g11: Another coordinated phrase: 'with our own things and with foreign ones.' Cose is a generic plural noun 'things/devices'; nostrali (local/native) and istrane (foreign/strange) are adjectives modifying cose. The repetition of con stresses variety of equipment/signs.
segments.inferno-22-g12: This is a negative correlative construction: né ... né ... ('neither ... nor ...'). Già here works adverbially with negative sense ('ever/already' in negative contexts = 'ever'). Con sì diversa cennamella is an instrumental/attendant phrase ('with so varied a shawm/pipe'); cennamella is an archaic wind instrument. Vidi muover repeats the witnessing structure: 'I had not (ever) seen ... move.'
segments.inferno-22-g13: Continuation of the negative correlative: 'nor a ship by sign of land or star.' A segno di terra o di stella is an idiomatic navigation expression ('by reference to land or to a guiding star'), with a expressing the means ('by').
segments.inferno-22-g14: Andavam is the imperfect indicative of andare (we were going), expressing ongoing past action; Noi (we) is an explicit subject pronoun for emphasis. Con li diece demoni is a prepositional phrase indicating accompaniment; li is the archaic article for the plural.
segments.inferno-22-g15: Ahi is an interjection expressing dismay ('ah'/'alas'). Compagnia is a noun meaning a company or group; fiera here is used adjectivally to characterize the company as fierce/wild (an adjective sense not directly listed in the provided senses), functioning as an exclamatory nominal phrase.
segments.inferno-22-g16: Ma introduces contrast ('but'). Ne la chiesa is an archaic prepositional phrase where ne functions like nella ('in the') and places the action/location 'in the church'; coi santi ('with the saints') is a prepositional phrase of accompaniment using the contracted form coi (= con + i).
segments.inferno-22-g17: Parallel structure to the previous half-line: e in taverna ('and in tavern/inn' — article omitted for poetic concision) followed by coi ghiottoni ('with the gluttons'). The preposition and contraction function the same as above, giving two contrasted social settings.
segments.inferno-22-g65: Tra + noun = 'among' here; 'male gatte' is a noun phrase with 'male' as an adjectival qualifier meaning 'bad/evil' modifying 'gatte' (female cats). 'Era venuto' is a compound past formed with the auxiliary essere + past participle venuto, here rendering a past action ('had come' / 'had arrived' or simply 'came' in narrative). '’l sorco' is the subject (the definite article + noun) and follows the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.inferno-22-g66: The coordinating conjunction 'ma' = 'but' begins the clause. 'Barbariccia' is the subject; 'il' is an object pronoun (here 'him/it') referring back to the swine. 'Chiuse' is the preterite (passato remoto/passato) of 'chiudere' meaning 'shut' or 'enclosed.' 'Con le braccia' is an instrumental phrase 'with his arms' (con + plural definite article + noun). The clause ends with 'e disse' ('and said'), linking to the following quoted speech.
segments.inferno-22-g67: 'State' here is an imperative plural (stàte) meaning 'stay' / 'stand back' addressed to the others; the form looks like the modern plural imperative of 'stare'. 'In là' is an adverbial phrase 'over there' / 'back there.' In poetry the imperative is often used abruptly without an explicit subject.
segments.inferno-22-g68: 'Mentr’' is the contracted form of 'mentre' = 'while.' 'Io' is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I.' 'Lo' is the direct object pronoun 'him/it' (referring to the swine). '’nforco' is an archaic/poetic, elided form of 'inforco' (from inforcare) in the present tense 'I impale/place on a fork' — the apostrophe marks the dropped initial vowel. The whole clause = 'while I impale him.'
segments.inferno-22-g69: The coordinating 'E' = 'and.' 'Al' is the contraction of 'a + il' = 'to the'; it introduces the indirect object 'maestro mio' = 'my master' (Virgil), with 'mio' after the noun as a possessive. 'Volse' is the past tense (preterite) of 'volgere' meaning 'turned'; 'la faccia' = 'the face' is the direct object, so the clause literally: 'And he turned his face to my master.'
segments.inferno-22-g70: A single quoted word 'Domanda' functions as an imperative or exhortation here: literally 'Question!' or 'Ask!' — an ellipsis for 'ask him.' In the narrative it introduces that he invites a question to be posed.
segments.inferno-22-g71: 'Disse' is the simple past (preterite) of 'dire' = 'said,' marking reported speech. It follows the quoted imperative to attribute the utterance.
segments.inferno-22-g72: 'Ancor' (ancora) = 'still' / 'yet' — an adverb that here qualifies the exhortation, meaning 'ask again' or 'ask further.' It's an archaic spelling but common in Dante.
segments.inferno-22-g73: This conditional/subordinate phrase breaks down as: 'se' = 'if' (introducing the condition), 'più' = 'any more' / 'more,' 'disii' is an older first-person singular past tense of 'desiderare' or 'disiderare' ('I desired/wished') — here used impersonally in sense 'if you desire further knowledge' (Dante's wording is compressed), 'saper' is the infinitive 'to learn/know' (here 'to learn'), 'da lui' = 'from him.' Syntactically the clause offers the condition under which one should 'ask.'
segments.inferno-22-g74: 'Prima' = 'before.' 'Ch’' is the contracted 'che' introducing the subordinate clause 'prima che ...' meaning 'before others ...'. 'Altri' = 'others' (the other devils). 'L' = the object pronoun 'him/it' (here written as l'), and 'disfaccia' is the present subjunctive of 'disfare' (or present of poetic usage) meaning 'undo' / 'tear apart' / 'destroy.' The clause = 'before others tear him apart.'
segments.inferno-22-g75: 'Lo' is the emphatic definite article 'the' (here used before titles); 'duca' = 'leader/duke' (Virgil as guide). 'Dunque' = 'then' / 'therefore' and here marks the turn of address: 'The leader, then (said):'.
segments.inferno-22-g76: 'Or' = 'now' introducing the direct question. 'Dì' is an imperative of 'dire' ('say' / 'tell'), used here to command that someone answer or speak. In Dante the imperative can appear in this abbreviated form.
segments.inferno-22-g77: This interrogative clause: 'de li altri rii' = 'of the other wicked ones' (or 'of the other streams' in other contexts; here 'rii' functions as a noun meaning 'guilty/wicked ones'). 'Conosci tu' = 'do you know' / 'do you recognize' (present tense, second person singular). 'Che sia latino' = relative clause with subjunctive 'sia' = 'who is Latin' (i.e., from Latium or speaking Latin). 'Sotto la pece' = 'under the pitch' (locative phrase describing where they are punished). The order is typical: prepositional phrase + main verb + interrogative/relative clause.
segments.inferno-22-g78: This segment is punctuation (a period) marking the end of the sentence; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-22-g122: This segment contains only a punctuation mark (a period) and no lexical material to analyse grammatically.
segments.inferno-22-g123: "Se voi volete vedere o udire" is a conditional/optative opening: Se (if) + voi (2nd-person plural subject) + volete (present indicative of volere) expresses a polite conditional 'if you want'. The infinitives vedere and udire follow volere in the usual Italian pattern 'volere + infinitive' meaning 'want to [do]'.
segments.inferno-22-g124: Ricominciò is the passato remoto (or literary simple past) of ricominciare used here as a reportive past — 'resumed' or 'began again'. Lo spaürato is a nominal phrase with the article lo + adjective used substantively ('the frightened one'); appresso is an adverb meaning 'nearby' or 'close at hand' modifying where/when he resumed.
segments.inferno-22-g125: A direct address: Toschi o Lombardi is vocative-plural naming the groups addressed. Toschi and Lombardi are plural nouns (or ethnonyms) joined by the coordinating conjunction o 'or' used here like 'Tuscans or Lombards' in direct speech.
segments.inferno-22-g126: Io ne farò venire combines the subject pronoun io (emphatic) with the future farò (from fare) + infinitive venir(e) to mean 'I will cause to come / I will bring'. The clitic ne refers back to people mentioned ("of them"/"some of them").
segments.inferno-22-g127: Ma stieno i Malebranche un poco in cesso is an imperative/request-like construction with stieno (3rd-person plural subjunctive/imperative in older/poetic usage meaning 'let them stay' or 'remain') followed by the subject i Malebranche. Un poco in cesso is an adverbial phrase 'a little in the privy' (cesso = privy/latrine here).
segments.inferno-22-g128: Sì ch’ei non teman de le lor vendette uses sì ch’ (so that) to introduce a result clause: ei (they) non teman (present subjunctive here because of volition/negated fearing) de le lor vendette = 'they do not fear their revenge'. Lor is the enclitic possessive (their).
segments.inferno-22-g129: E io is simply the coordinating conjunction e 'and' plus the emphatic subject pronoun io 'I', introducing the speaker's personal action next.
segments.inferno-22-g130: Seggendo in questo loco stesso uses the present participle seggendo (from seggere, an archaic form for 'sitting') to mean 'sitting'. In questo loco stesso is a demonstrative adverbial phrase 'in this very place' (stesso = 'same/very').
segments.inferno-22-g131: Per un ch’io son is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic clause: per un 'because' or 'for one' introduces a causal/explanatory phrase; ch'io son = 'that I am' (son = 'am'). The whole means roughly 'because I am one' (i.e., being one of them / in that capacity).
segments.inferno-22-g132: Ne farò venir sette quand’ io suffolerò contains ne (of them/some of them) + future farò + infinitive venir 'will make come' and a temporal clause quand' io suffolerò ('when I shall blow/when I blow the signal'); suffolerò is future tense of an archaic verb suffolare meaning to blow (a horn/signal) or to give the signal.
segments.inferno-22-g133: Com’ è nostro uso di fare allor che fori alcun si mette is a formulaic closing: com' è nostro uso 'as is our custom' + di fare 'to do' + allor che 'when/whenever' + fori alcun si mette (an archaic phrase where fori corresponds to 'fuori' = 'outside' and alcun si mette = 'anyone puts himself/comes out'). The clause explains that the blowing is the usual practice when someone goes outside.
segments.inferno-22-g134: Cagnazzo is a proper name (the demon). A cotal motto = at such a saying/remark (a introduces the phrase 'at' or 'in reaction to'); levò is the passato remoto of levare 'to lift' and ’l muso = the snout/face (the article l + muso).
segments.inferno-22-g135: Crollando ’l capo uses the present participle crollando ('shaking') with the object ’l capo ('the head') to indicate the simultaneous action 'shaking his head'. The participle gives a descriptive subordinate action.
segments.inferno-22-g136: E disse is simply the connective e 'and' plus disse, passato remoto of dire, reporting his spoken words: 'and he said'. In direct speech that follows, punctuation marks the quoted speech.
segments.inferno-22-g137: Odi malizia ch’elli ha pensata per gittarsi giuso! is an exclamatory clause: Odi (imperative or present subjunctive used vocatively/command) = 'hear' or 'mark' + malizia 'wickedness/plot' + ch'elli ha pensata 'that he has devised' (ha pensata = perfect tense with pensata functioning as past participle or adjectival complement) + per gittarsi giuso 'to throw himself down / to plunge down' where gittarsi is a pronominal verb 'to throw oneself' and giuso = 'down'.
segments.inferno-22-g138: This segment is only punctuation (a period) and contains no words for grammatical analysis.
segments.inferno-22-g158: "Lo Navarrese" is a proper nickname-like phrase with the definite article lo + gentilic (the Navarrese). "ben" is an adverb modifying the phrase "suo tempo" (literally "his time/moment") and intensifies it: "at just the right moment." "colse" is the passato remoto of cogliere used here in the sense 'seized' or 'grasped' (an opportunity). Word order (adverb + possessive + noun) is idiomatic/poetic.
segments.inferno-22-g159: "fermò" is the passato remoto of fermare meaning 'fixed/placed/firmly set' (here: set down/plant). The direct object "le piante" means 'the soles (of the feet)' (piante = soles) and follows the verb; "a terra" is a prepositional phrase 'to the ground' indicating where he set them.
segments.inferno-22-g160: The coordinating conjunction "e" links actions. "in un punto" is an adverbial phrase ('in a moment/instant'). "saltò" is passato remoto of saltare ('he leapt/jumped'). The second clause "e dal proposto lor si sciolse": "dal" = da + il ('from the'), "proposto" is a noun/participle used as a person/thing ('the one appointed/put forward' or agent of the restraint), "lor" = 'their', and "si sciolse" is reflexive passato remoto of sciogliere ('he freed himself, broke away'). The reflexive "si" shows the action returns to the subject.
segments.inferno-22-g161: "Di che" here means 'by which / at this' and links back to the preceding action; it introduces the result. "ciascun" (ciascuno) is 'each one'. The prepositional phrase "di colpa" indicates the cause/quality ('with guilt' or 'by guilt'). "fu compunto" is verbo essere + past participle (passive/state) in passato remoto: 'was stricken/was contrite/was pierced with remorse.'
segments.inferno-22-g162: "ma" = 'but'. "quei" is a demonstrative/pronoun ('that one' / 'he') referring to a particular person (the Navarrese). "più" ('more') sets up a comparison; "che" here is a relative 'who/that' introducing the relative clause "che cagion fu del difetto" ('who was the cause of the fault'). "cagion fu del difetto" is a nominal clause with cagion 'cause' + fu (past of essere) + del difetto 'of the fault'.
segments.inferno-22-g163: "però" is a consequence/continuative adverb ('therefore, so'). "si mosse" is passato remoto of muoversi (reflexive) meaning 'he moved/stirred himself' (i.e., he set himself in motion). "e gridò" continues with another passato remoto verb 'and he cried/shouted.'
segments.inferno-22-g164: Direct speech. "Tu" is the pronoun 'you'. "se’" is an archaic or contracted form representing "sei" (second-person singular present of essere) and with the past participle "giunto" yields the idiomatic 'you have arrived' (sei giunto) — present perfect sense in modern English rendered here as 'you have arrived' or simply 'you're here.'
segments.inferno-22-g165: This segment is a punctuation mark (period) marking the end of the preceding sentence; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-22-g166: Ma is the simple conjunction 'but'. poco is an adverb modifying the verb 'valse' and means 'little' or 'of little use'. i is an enclitic indirect-object pronoun 'to them' referring back to the creatures already mentioned; valse is valse (valere) in the passato remoto (third person singular) meaning 'it availed' or 'it was of use' — together: 'But it availed them little.'
segments.inferno-22-g167: ché is the causal conjunction 'for'/'because'. l' ali is the subject 'the wings' (l' is the article used before a vowel in poetry). al sospetto is an idiomatic locution meaning 'at the alarm/fear' (al = 'to/the' + sospetto = 'alarm/fear'). non potero avanzar: potero is an archaic/passé remoto form meaning 'were able' (3rd person plural to agree with ali), non negates it, and avanzar is the infinitive 'to advance'.
segments.inferno-22-g168: quelli here functions as a demonstrative pronoun 'that one' (one of the two previously mentioned). andò is andare in passato remoto (3rd person singular) 'went'. sotto is an adverb 'under'/'down' — so 'that one went down/under.'
segments.inferno-22-g169: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. quei is the other demonstrative pronoun 'the other one'. drizzò is drizzare in passato remoto (3rd person singular) meaning 'raised'/'straightened'; volando is a gerund 'flying' modifying the action; suso is an adverb 'upward' (here marking direction) — the sequence drizzò volando suso il petto means 'raised, while flying, its breast upward' with il petto as the direct object of drizzò.
segments.inferno-22-g170: non altrimenti = 'not otherwise' / 'just so'. l'anitra is the definite article + noun 'the duck'. di botto is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'suddenly' ('di' combines with botto to make the adverbial expression). The clause sets up a simile: 'not otherwise the duck, suddenly...'.
segments.inferno-22-g171: quando is the temporal conjunction 'when'. l' falcon = 'the falcon' (poetic article before a consonant omitted in writing). s'appressa is the pronominal verb 'si appressa' (3rd person singular present) meaning 'approaches' or 'draws near' — the reflexive clitic s' marks the intransitive pronominal construction.
segments.inferno-22-g172: giù is an adverb 'down'. s' is the reflexive clitic (si) and attuffa is the present indicative (3rd person singular) of attuffarsi 'to plunge/dives in'; the inversion giù s'attuffa places the adverb before the verb: 'it plunges down.'
segments.inferno-22-g173: ed is the form of e 'and' used before a vowel. ei is an archaic subject pronoun 'it'/'he' referring to the attacker (the falcon) here. ritorna is present indicative 'returns' (3rd person singular). sù is the adverb 'up'. crucciato e rotto are past participles used predicatively to describe the state on returning: 'angry/upset and broken/wounded.'
segments.inferno-22-g107: Danar is fronted as the direct object ('money'). 'si tolse' is passato remoto of togliersi with the reflexive pronoun si: 'he took for himself' (literally 'took money to himself'). 'e lasciolli' contains the enclitic pronoun -li attached to lasciò (old spelling lasciolli) meaning 'and left them/left them alone.' 'di piano' is an adverbial phrase ('quietly/softly/gradually') introduced by di.
segments.inferno-22-g108: Sì = 'so' or 'thus'; com' is com(e) with elision and means 'as'. E' here is the clitic contraction of egli ('he'), not the conjunction 'and' (the provided lemma list lacks that sense). Dice is present indicative 'says.' Together: 'as he says.'
segments.inferno-22-g109: E = 'and'. Ne li altri offici = 'in/among the other offices' (ne = 'in/of them'; li = definite article before altro in archaic order). Anche adds 'also.' 'Barattier fu non picciol' is an archaic word order: fu (passato remoto 'was') + non picciol ('not small' = 'not insignificant').
segments.inferno-22-g110: Ma = 'but/rather' introducing a contrast; sovrano is a postpositive adjective (elliptical: '(he was) sovereign/supreme'), modifying the preceding subject 'barattier'.
segments.inferno-22-g111: Usa here is third-person singular present of usare in the sense 'keeps company with / associates with.' Con esso donno = 'with that lord' (esso = 'that/him'); di Logodoro names his domain ('of Logudoro').
segments.inferno-22-g112: E = 'and.' 'A dir di Sardigna' is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'when it comes to speaking of Sardinia' (a + infinitive = 'in speaking of'). 'Le lingue lor' = 'their tongues' (le lingue subject, lor = their). 'Non si sentono stanche' uses the pronominal verb sentirsi (present) 'do not feel (themselves) tired'; stanche agrees feminine plural with lingue.
segments.inferno-22-g113: Omè is an interjection (archaic) meaning 'ah me' or 'alas.'
segments.inferno-22-g114: Vedete is vocative/imperative or present 'you see/behold'; l’altro = 'the other'; che introduces a relative clause 'who'; digrigna (from digrignare) is present indicative 'gnashes/grinds (his) teeth' describing ongoing action.
segments.inferno-22-g115: I' here is an archaic or dialectal form for io ('I') not represented in the provided senses list. Direi is first-person conditional 'I would say'; anche = 'also.'
segments.inferno-22-g116: Ma = 'but.' I' (archaic 'io') + temo = 'I fear.' Ch'ello = che + egli ('that he'). The subordinate verb apparecchi is in the present subjunctive (temo che ... apparecchi) expressing fear; s' is the reflexive pronoun (si) 'himself.' Non negates the subjunctive. 'A grattarmi' uses the infinitive grattare with enclitic -mi ('to scratch me'); la tigna = 'the scab/ringworm' (literal or contemptuous). Note some listed lemmas are nominal senses while the form here is verbal or pronominal, hence some senses are not in the provided list.
segments.inferno-22-g117: E = 'and.' L' gran proposto = 'the great proposto'; proposto here is an office-holder title (the provost/leader of the devils) rather than the literal senses like 'proposed' or 'purpose.'
segments.inferno-22-g118: Vòlto (past participle used as a verbal adjective) = 'turned' or 'facing,' followed by a (to) + Farfarello (proper name). The relative clause 'che stralunava li occhi per fedire' = 'who was rolling his eyes in order to strike/wound': stralunava is imperfect (ongoing action), li occhi is the object with archaic preposed article, and 'per' expresses purpose ('to/for the purpose of').
segments.inferno-22-g119: Disse is passato remoto (third-person singular) of dire: 'he said.' It introduces direct speech.
segments.inferno-22-g120: Fatti 'n costà is an imperative/reflexive idiom: fatti is the imperative (or reflexive form of fare) meaning roughly 'make yourself' or colloquially 'get yourself' (i.e. 'get over there'); '’n' is elided in for in; costà = 'over there/away there.' The listed noun senses for Fatti do not match this verbal/imparative usage, so the provided sense is not applicable.
segments.inferno-22-g121: Malvagio uccello is a direct insult in vocative: malvagio = 'wicked/wretched' modifying uccello = 'bird' used figuratively as a term of reproach. The exclamation closes the quoted command.
segments.inferno-22-g92: Subject + adverb + (archaic) object pronoun + passato remoto volere + infinitive construction. Draghignazzo is the subject; anco = 'also'. The clitic i is an archaic form for 'them' placed before the verb (here not listed correctly in the sense list). volle is passato remoto of volere ('he wanted') and takes the infinitive dar ('to give' = 'to deal/inflict'); dar di piglio is an idiomatic phrase 'to give a grip/take hold'. Giuso modifies the following prepositional phrase a le gambe = 'down at the legs'.
segments.inferno-22-g93: Onde introduces a consequence ('whereupon/therefore'). 'Il decurio loro' = 'their decurion' (possessive following the noun, a common older ordering). si volse is reflexive passato remoto of volgere ('he turned himself/turned around'). The doubled intorno intorno is a poetic/colloquial reduplication meaning 'all around' or 'about all round'. Con mal piglio is an ablative/with-phrase meaning 'with a bad/ill bearing/look' (mal piglio = bad expression or ill intent).
segments.inferno-22-g94: Temporal clause introduced by Quand' (apocopated quando) = 'when'. elli is an archaic subject pronoun meaning 'they' (the form in the senses list is incorrect); un poco = 'a little'; rappaciati is a past participle ('reconciled/pacified') used predicatively; fuoro is the archaic passato remoto 3rd plural of essere (furono) = 'they were'.
segments.inferno-22-g95: Simple prepositional phrase 'a lui' = 'to him' (dative), detached to indicate who is being addressed or looked at.
segments.inferno-22-g96: Relative clause introduced by ch' = che/who. ancor = 'still'; mirava is imperfect indicative of mirare = 'was gazing at/looking at'; sua ferita = 'his wound' (possessive + noun). The imperfect stresses a continuing action at the time of questioning.
segments.inferno-22-g97: Main verb domandò is passato remoto of domandare = 'he asked'. 'Il duca mio' = 'my duke/guide' (possessive after title), and sanza dimoro is an idiom 'without delay' (sanza = 'without'; dimoro here functions as an archaic noun meaning 'delay/lingering').
segments.inferno-22-g98: Direct question in quoted speech. Chi fu colui = 'Who was that one' (fu = passato remoto 'was'). 'da cui mala partita' is a relative phrase 'from whom (a) bad parting' (mala partita = 'bad separation/ill parting' — archaic noun use); di' is the imperative 'di' = dì = 'say/tell' (written with an apostrophe here), introducing the command 'tell (me)'; che facesti per venire a proda? = 'what did you do in order to come to the shore?' where che facesti is an interrogative clause and per + infinitive expresses purpose 'to come'.
segments.inferno-22-g99: Punctuation mark only; this segment is the printed period that closes the verse/question and separates the narrative from the reported reply.
segments.inferno-22-g100: Ed = 'and' (conjunction). ei is an archaic nominative pronoun meaning 'he' (here 'he answered'); rispuose is passato remoto of rispondere = 'answered/replied'. The colon introduces the reported speech that follows.
segments.inferno-22-g101: Reported speech begins. Fu is passato remoto of essere = 'it/he was' (used to identify); frate = 'friar/brother' (title); Gomita is a proper name. The simple structure identifies the person named.
segments.inferno-22-g102: Quel di Gallura = 'that one of/from Gallura' (quel = 'that one'; di indicates origin or association, here 'of Gallura'). This is a short appositive identifying his region.
segments.inferno-22-g103: Vasel d'ogne froda is a figurative expression: vasel (a small vessel/receptacle) + d' (di) + ogne (archaic for ogni = 'every') + froda (fraud/deceit). The phrase means 'a vessel full of every deceit' (i.e. thoroughly deceitful). Note the contracted d' and the archaic ogne.
segments.inferno-22-g104: Relative clause introduced by ch' = che/who. ebbe is passato remoto of avere = 'he had'. i nemici = 'the enemies'. di suo donno = 'of his lord/master' (suo = his; donno = lord/master). in mano = idiom 'in hand' = 'in his power'. The clause says he held his lord's enemies in his power.
segments.inferno-22-g105: e fé sì lor: e = 'and'; fé is an archaic/historical form of fare in the passato remoto meaning 'he did/made'; sì = 'so/thus'; lor = clitic for 'to them' (archaic for 'loro'). The phrase means 'and he did so to them' or 'and he treated them in such a way'.
segments.inferno-22-g106: Result clause introduced by che = 'that/so that'. ciascun = 'each one/everybody' is the subject. se is a reflexive clitic (here representing 'themselves' in the pronominal construction se ne loda); ne = 'of it/about it' (refers back to what was done); loda is present indicative of lodare = 'praises/boasts'. The combined se ne loda means 'each one boasts of it' or 'everyone praises him for it'.
segments.inferno-22-g174: A nominative phrase naming the demon: 'Irato' is an adjective placed before the proper name (literally 'angry'); 'Calcabrina' is the proper name; 'de la' is an older form of 'di + la' = 'of the', introducing an epithet; 'buffa' is the noun of the epithet (here 'buffa' functions as a nickname, 'the Buffa'/'of the jest').
segments.inferno-22-g175: 'Volando' is a gerund (indicating manner: 'flying'); 'dietro' is an adverb meaning 'behind/after'; the clitic 'li' is a third-person plural direct object pronoun placed before the finite verb; 'tenne' is passato remoto of 'tenere' meaning here 'kept' or 'kept close / followed'.
segments.inferno-22-g176: 'Invaghito' is a past participle used adjectivally ('taken with' / 'infatuated'); the conjunction 'che' introduces a subordinate clause; 'quei' ('that one') is a demonstrative referring to the other creature; 'campasse' is the imperfect subjunctive of 'campare' (here expressing the wished-for/possible event: 'might live / survive'); 'per' + infinitive expresses purpose ('in order to'); 'aver' is the infinitive 'avere' ('have' used with 'la zuffa' = 'to get/obtain the fight'); 'la zuffa' = 'the brawl'.
segments.inferno-22-g177: Coordinating conjunction 'e' ('and'); 'come' here functions temporally/causally ('when' / 'as'); '’l barattier' = 'the barrator' (a sinner); 'fu' is passato remoto of 'essere' ('was' / auxiliary); 'disparito' is past participle of 'disparire' used intransitively = 'disappeared'.
segments.inferno-22-g178: 'Così' ('thus/so') links this action to the previous; 'volse' is passato remoto of 'volgere' meaning 'turned' or 'directed'; the clitic 'li' here marks the definite direct object 'the' before 'artigli' (the talons); 'artigli' is the object 'talons/claws'; 'al' = 'a + il' ('to the'); 'suo compagno' = 'his companion' (possessive + noun).
segments.inferno-22-g179: Coordinating 'e' ('and'); 'fu' is passato remoto of 'essere' used with the predicative past participle 'ghermito' ('was seized/snatched'); 'con lui' specifies with whom; 'sopra' is the preposition 'on/over'; '’l fosso' = 'the ditch'; word order places the location after the person: 'was seized with him over the ditch.'
segments.inferno-22-g180: 'Ma' = 'but'; 'l’altro' = 'the other/one'; 'fu' is passato remoto of 'essere' ('was'); 'bene' here intensifies ('truly'/'very'); 'sparvier' is 'sparrowhawk' (a predatory bird image); 'grifagno' is an adjective meaning 'rapacious' or 'hawk-like'; 'ad' + infinitive ('ad artigliar') indicates purpose or aptitude ('to seize/with the aim to claw'); the final 'ben lui' ('well him') emphasizes that he was effective at seizing him.
segments.inferno-22-g181: Coordinating 'e' ('and'); 'amendue' = 'both' (both of them); 'cadder' is passato remoto third-person plural of 'cadere' ('they fell'); 'nel mezzo del' = 'into the middle of the'; 'bogliente' (archaic spelling of 'bollente') = 'boiling, seething'; 'stagno' = 'pond/pool' (here the boiling pool of the Malebolge).
segments.inferno-22-g182: 'Lo' is the masculine definite article; 'caldo' ('hot') qualifies the following noun; 'sghermitor' is an agent noun formed from 'sghermire' (one who releases or frees); 'sùbito' is an adverb 'immediately/suddenly'; 'fue' is passato remoto of 'essere' ('was')—together: 'the hot releaser was immediately there.'
segments.inferno-22-g183: 'ma però' ('but nevertheless'); 'di levarsi' is an infinitive with reflexive pronoun expressing the attempt 'to lift oneself / to get up'; 'era' is imperfect of 'essere' ('it was'); 'neente' (archaic 'niente') means 'nothing / of no use'—the whole clause means 'but it was of no use to try to rise.'
segments.inferno-22-g184: 'sì' ('so/thus') introduces the result; 'avieno' is the imperfect third-person plural of 'avere' ('they had'); 'inviscate' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'stuck with bird-lime/glued'; 'l’ ali sue' = 'his wings' (article + noun + possessive). Grammatically this says the wings had been glued, explaining why rising was impossible.
segments.inferno-22-g185: A single name in apposition: 'Barbariccia' (the leader demon) standing as the subject of the following actions.
segments.inferno-22-g186: 'con' = 'with'; 'li altri suoi' = 'his other ones' (his other followers/comrades); 'dolente' is an adjective ('sorrowful' or 'grieving') describing them—word order is poetic, with adjective after the noun-phrase.
segments.inferno-22-g187: 'quattro ne fé volar' is a causative construction: 'he made four of them fly'—'ne' is the clitic partitive pronoun referring to 'of them' (those on the bank); 'fé' is passato remoto of 'fare' in the causative sense ('made'); 'volar' is the infinitive 'to fly'; 'da l’altra costa' = 'from the other bank/side'; 'con tutt’ i raffi' literally 'with all the rafte/strokes' (an image of vigorous swoops).
segments.inferno-22-g188: Coordinating 'e' ('and'); 'assai prestamente' = 'very quickly' (adverb + adverb); 'di qua' = 'from here'—an adverbial phrase indicating direction/origination.
segments.inferno-22-g189: 'di là' = 'from there'; 'discesero' is passato remoto (third plural) of 'discendere' = 'they descended' or 'came down'; 'a la posta' = 'to the post/position/spot' (the place they were to take up).
segments.inferno-22-g190: 'Porser' is passato remoto of 'porgere' ('they stretched/held out'); 'li uncini' = 'the hooks' (direct object); 'verso' = 'toward'; 'li ’mpaniati' = 'the bird-limed ones' (those stuck with bird-lime)—the clause describes the action of holding hooks toward the trapped sinners.
segments.inferno-22-g191: Relative clause introduced by 'ch’' (= 'che', 'who/which/that'); 'eran' is imperfect of 'essere' ('they were'); 'già' = 'already'; 'cotti' is past participle 'cooked' (here meaning their bodies were being cooked); 'dentro' = 'inside'; 'da la crosta' = 'to the crust' or 'by the crust' (the outer layer already cooked)—descriptive result of the boiling pit.
